nodes.summary: Pure grayscale-fraction measurement over an ARGB pixel buffer, used by content-type auto-detection to distinguish B/W manga interiors from coloured comics.

nodes.summary: Returns the fraction (0..1) of ARGB pixels whose channel span is below satEps, i.e. effectively grayscale; empty buffer yields 0 (undecidable).

nodes.summary: Unit tests for resolveHyphenationLang covering auto-resolution from document language, region-tag normalization, explicit overrides, and off/unsupported fallbacks.

nodes.name: ApplyReaderPresetTest.kt

nodes.summary: Unit tests for the novel word-bookmark logic: nextBookmarkNumber (max+1, no gap reuse) and toggleBookmark set/remove semantics.

nodes.name: cr3_bridge.cpp

nodes.name: nativeSearch

nodes.name: CrengineDocumentFactory.kt

layers.name: Domain Core
layers.description: Pure-Kotlin heart of the app: data models, use-cases, ViewerType resolution, and the repository/render/E-Ink interface contracts that depend on neither Android nor any concrete source.

layers.name: Seam A — Sources
layers.description: The source-agnostic backend seam: the MediaSource/BrowsableSource contract (source-api) plus the Komga REST, OPDS, and local SAF-folder adapter implementations behind it.

layers.name: Seam B — Render & Device
layers.description: The render/device seam: MuPDF page rendering (render-core), crengine EPUB reflow with the cr3_bridge JNI (render-crengine), and the Onyx E-Ink controller (eink-onyx) behind the Document/EinkController interfaces.

layers.name: Plugin System
layers.description: Runtime extensibility stack: the plugin ABI contract (plugin-api), the TOFU-pinning PathClassLoader APK loader (plugin-host), and the shaded single-jar SDK (plugin-sdk) that external plugins compile against.

layers.name: UI Modularity Contracts
layers.description: The UI-pack seam: slot/shell/theme/icon capability contracts plus the decoupled built-in theme packs and icon stack, sitting between domain and the app shell (domain → ui-api → app).

layers.name: Data & Persistence
layers.description: Room database implementations, the offline-first sync queue, collection sync, the plugin repo client, and the download manager — the persistence layer below the UI.

layers.name: App Shell
layers.description: The imperative shell: Compose UI, ViewModels, Hilt DI wiring, the reader host, and the coupled default UI packs/renderers that wire every seam together.

layers.name: Tests
layers.description: Unit, integration, and instrumented Android tests across all modules — domain/mapper unit tests, source contract tests, and the CI fixture-driven E2E suites under androidTest.

layers.name: Documentation
layers.description: Project documentation: design specs, implementation plans, and UI-pack guides under docs/, plus the root project guide (CLAUDE.md), README, CONTRIBUTING, and license NOTICE.

layers.name: Project Tooling & Build
layers.description: Build and developer tooling: Gradle build scripts and version catalog, the wrapper, Android manifests, icon/CI tooling under tools/, the GitLab and GitHub CI pipelines, and the Docker fixture services.

tour.title: Project Vision & Two Seams
tour.description: Start with the README to learn what Komga Reader is: a native, E-Ink-first Android reader for Onyx Boox that unifies paged comics, webtoon scrolling, and EPUB reflow against multiple backends. The root CLAUDE.md is the architectural constitution — it states the five non-negotiable invariants, the two load-bearing seams (Seam A = sources, Seam B = render/device), and the rule that all variability lives behind interfaces. ARCHITECTURE.md draws the module map so you can see how every later step fits together.

tour.title: Domain Core: Models & ViewerType
tour.description: The domain module is pure Kotlin with no Android, network, or source dependencies — it is the vocabulary the whole app speaks. Series and Book are the source-neutral metadata models every adapter fills in as best it can, while ContentType and ResolveViewerType encode the deterministic rule (override > shelf type > ViewerType) that decides which reader opens a work. Understanding these types first makes every downstream layer legible.

tour.title: Domain Contracts: Repositories & E-Ink
tour.description: Building on the models, the domain also owns the interface contracts that keep concrete implementations out of the core. SettingsRepository (one of the highest fan-in files in the project) and ServerRepository define persistence boundaries the data layer must satisfy, while EinkController abstracts the physical device so development never crashes on non-Boox hardware. These interfaces are the inward-facing half of both seams.

tour.title: Seam A: The Source Contract
tour.description: Seam A is where all backend variability is captured. MediaSource (and its BrowsableSource/SyncingSource refinements) is the single contract every backend implements — it is the second-highest fan-in file in the codebase, which is why the UI and domain may only ever depend on it, never on a concrete source. SourceManager is the registry that hands out sources by their stable, deterministic id at runtime.

tour.title: Seam A: Concrete Source Adapters
tour.description: These three adapters prove the seam holds: KomgaSource speaks the Komga REST API, OpdsSource parses an OPDS feed, and LocalSource exposes a user-picked SAF device folder — all behind the same MediaSource interface, none knowing about the others. Note how LocalSource is renderer-free (it returns raw zip entries for CBZ and falls back to whole-file rendering for PDF/EPUB) and uses Base64-opaque remoteIds so device paths survive navigation routing.

tour.title: Seam B: Rendering Documents
tour.description: Seam B turns bytes into pixels, kept strictly separate from the view. Document/DocumentFactory in the domain is the render contract; MupdfDocument backs it with the MuPDF JNI engine for CBZ/CBR/PDF and EPUB, while CrengineDocument provides reflowable EPUB via the cr3_bridge native bridge. Swapping or adding an engine means a new implementation behind this interface, never a core rewrite.
tour.languageLesson: The cr3_bridge.cpp JNI layer wraps the C++ crengine library: Kotlin declares external native methods, and JNI marshals calls across the boundary. Native method signatures are part of an ABI contract — renaming a Kotlin external fun without updating the C++ symbol breaks linkage at runtime, not compile time.

tour.title: Seam B: The Onyx E-Ink Controller
tour.description: OnyxEinkController is the device half of Seam B — the concrete EinkController for Boox hardware, hardware-gated by Build.MANUFACTURER so it only engages on real devices. It maps logical refresh and color modes onto the Onyx EpdController/EinkWise SDK per reading context (paged, webtoon, novel), realizing the E-Ink design language's promise that refresh is steered deliberately rather than blindly invalidated.

tour.title: The Plugin System
tour.description: Phase 4's runtime extensibility stack lets users install sources and presets as separate APKs. SourcePlugin is the pure-JVM ABI contract a plugin implements; PluginHost is the loader that discovers plugin packages, gates them by a two-integer ABI version, and instantiates them via a PathClassLoader only after a TOFU certificate pin matches. This is the same seam principle applied to third-party code — the contract is stable so plugins survive app updates.

tour.title: Modular UI Contracts (ui-api)
tour.description: Above the seams sits a three-layer modular UI, all contracts living in the ui-api module (DAG: domain → ui-api → app). UiSlots defines addressable Chrome regions (header, dialog, tiles, reader scaffold) that a pack can replace one by one; AppShellState is the whole home layout skeleton a shell pack rearranges by form factor; EinkTokens and AppIcons carry the host-enforced theme tokens and swappable icon registry. Packs supply content and structure, but motion and accent policy stay host-enforced.

tour.title: Data & Persistence Layer
tour.description: The data module implements the domain's repository interfaces and provides offline-first storage. AppDatabase is the Room schema (every row carries a sourceId so uninstalling a source falls back cleanly to a stub); RoomSettingsRepository backs the high-fan-in settings contract from Step 3; DownloadManager handles offline copies that the reader's load path prefers over streaming. This layer never depends on the UI.
tour.languageLesson: Room migrations must be deterministic and reversible. A subtle pitfall noted in this project: ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN with a default but without a matching @ColumnInfo defaultValue causes a destructive schema mismatch — schema versions and migration steps must stay in lockstep with the entity annotations.

tour.title: App Wiring: Source Registration & Sync
tour.description: The app module is the imperative shell where every seam is wired together — the only place concrete source types are allowed. SourceRegistration builds the SourceManager from saved server configs and is where Komga/OPDS/plugin specifics live; ActiveSource is the source-agnostic resolver every ViewModel injects (resolving per-work via get(sourceId), never "the first/active" one); SyncCoordinator is the central seam that drives full/pull sync and plugin discovery on app start and server changes.

tour.title: App Entry: MainActivity & Theme Host
tour.description: MainActivity is the application entry point and the top entry-point candidate by score: it sets up the Compose host, applies the resolved theme/UI pack, handles external "open with" VIEW intents, and classifies hardware button presses. UiPackTheme is the host that picks a theme pack by device class and lets an external data pack override it — host-gated so mono E-Ink always ignores accent color. This is the bird's-eye runtime structure everything else hangs from.
tour.languageLesson: Jetpack Compose is declarative: MainActivity calls setContent {} once, then UI is a function of state. CompositionLocals (LocalEinkMode, LocalDisplayBehavior, LocalResolvedSlots) flow ambient values down the tree so any composable can read device behavior or the active pack without threading them through every parameter.

tour.title: The Reader Host & ViewModels
tour.description: ReaderRoute is the dispatcher that maps the resolved ViewerMode/ReaderContent onto one of the reader screens, and ReaderViewModel chooses the source-agnostic load path: a local download renders whole-file via MuPDF, a streaming source pages through openPage, and an empty pages() list (OPDS, local PDF) falls back to whole-file. All reader ViewModels implement the shared Viewer state contract so the reader scaffold works against any of them.

tour.title: The Four Reader Screens
tour.description: The journey ends at the reading experience itself: four Compose reader engines, each a distinct mode but all sharing the ReaderScaffold chrome and Viewer seam (extracted before the Nth variant, per the project's shared-structure rule). PagedReaderScreen handles comics page-by-page, WebtoonReaderScreen renders the seamless vertical strip, ComicReaderScreen drives panel-by-panel guided navigation, and NovelReaderScreen hosts crengine EPUB reflow with typography and word bookmarks. These engines are Core — they carry the render/refresh correctness that no UI pack may replace.
